does: This paper develops AdaGRPO, an adaptive loss-balancing framework for reinforcement learning in generative recommendation that selectively applies reward signals based on per-sample conditions. The key insight is that naively applying GRPO (a policy gradient method) uniformly across all samples is harmful when the reward model—typically a biased production ranker—provides unreliable guidance. The authors propose gating the GRPO objective with a learnable binary mechanism that determines whether each sample should receive reward-based guidance or remain anchored in supervised learning. Through stratified empirical analysis, they identify that reward signals help most when the policy is uncertain and the ranker can discriminate ground-truth items effectively, while hurting performance otherwise. The framework treats RL as selective admission rather than uniform pressure, learning which samples benefit from reward guidance during training.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in applying RL to recommendation systems, particularly methods like GRPO that combine policy gradients with generative models. It directly addresses a critical limitation in production recommendation systems: the exposure bias and sample-dependent noise inherent in ranker-based reward models trained on logged interactions. The paper connects to broader concerns in RL about reward model misspecification and noisy reward signals, echoing work on robust RL and uncertainty-aware policy learning. It extends prior research questioning the uniform application of RL objectives by introducing an adaptive gating mechanism. The approach also relates to recent work on mixing supervised and reinforcement learning objectives, where selective application has shown benefits over pure RL in other domains.
enables: This framework opens directions for more principled integration of RL in production recommendation systems where reward models are inherently noisy. By enabling selective reward guidance, it paves the way for learning better criteria to identify trustworthy versus corrupted signals, potentially inspiring more sophisticated uncertainty estimation for reward models. The approach could extend to other domains where RL training faces similar reward corruption challenges. Future work might explore learned gating functions beyond binary classification, meta-learning methods to adapt the selection policy across different reward models, or theoretical analysis of when and why selective admission outperforms uniform approaches. This also suggests practical deployment strategies for gradually increasing RL reliance in systems as confidence in reward estimates grows.
